g.data-narration: Welcome to Sequential Search and Brute Force String Matching - fundamental algorithms in computer science.
g.text: Sequential Search & Brute Force String Matching
university - Analysis and Design of Algorithms

g.data-narration: Searching is foundational in computer science, powering databases, text editors, and internet search engines worldwide.
g.text: Searching is one of the most fundamental operations in computer science.

g.data-narration: We study two key algorithms: Sequential Search checks each element, while Brute Force matches patterns in text.
g.text: Examines each element in a list

g.data-narration: The sequential search algorithm compares each element with the target, returning the index when found or -1 if not found.
g.text: • Works on sorted AND unsorted arrays
• Returns index if found, -1 if not

g.data-narration: In this example, we search for 89 in array 23,45,67,12,89,34,56. We find it at index 4 after 5 comparisons.
Sequential search has O(1) best case, O(n) average, and O(n) worst case time complexity. Space complexity is O(1).
For sorted arrays, we can optimize by stopping early when current element exceeds the target, improving average performance.
g.text: Early termination when arr[i] > key saves comparisons!

g.data-narration: Brute force string matching slides a pattern across text, comparing all characters at each position until a match is found.
g.text: j = 0; while j < m and pattern[j] == text[i+j]: j++
• Shift pattern one position at a time

g.data-narration: In our example, pattern BAD is found at index 1 in text ABADBADABABAD after successful character comparison.

g.text: A B A D B A D A B A B A D

g.data-narration: String matching has O(m) best case, O(nm) average and worst case. The worst case occurs with patterns like AAA in texts like AAAAAA.
g.text: Space Complexity: O(1) for basic implementation

g.data-narration: Both algorithms share O(1) space complexity but differ in application: sequential search finds elements, string matching finds substrings.
g.text: Target: Element in array | Input: n elements
Target: Pattern in text | Input: n×m characters

g.data-narration: Use sequential search for small arrays under 50 elements or unsorted data where sorting would cost more than the search itself.
Avoid common mistakes: confusing linear search O(1) best case with binary search, forgetting the not-found return value, and off-by-one errors.
g.text: ✗ O(1) best case is NOT binary search
✗ Forgetting to return -1 for not found

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: sequential search is O(n) simple but effective; brute force string matching is O(nm) but easy to implement.
g.text: • Works on sorted and unsorted arrays

g.data-narration: For exams: memorize complexity formulas, practice counting comparisons, and know when to use brute force versus advanced algorithms.
g.text: ✓ Memorize time complexity formulas: Best/Average/Worst cases
✓ Practice counting comparisons: Sequential (n+1)/2 average, String matching nm worst
✓ Remember: Both use O(1) extra space - key advantage in memory-constrained environments
